sections.title: Two jobs — conjunctions vs linkers
sections.content:
Conjunctions and linkers are the **explicit machinery** that joins ideas. They do two distinct jobs:
- **Conjunctions** link words, phrases, or clauses **inside** a sentence: *and, but, because, although*.
- **Discourse linkers** connect **ideas across** sentences and paragraphs: *however, therefore, in addition*.

**The critical rule**: each word fits a specific **grammar slot**. Putting a sentence-linker (*however*) where a conjunction (*but*) belongs — or doubling them (*although … but*) — is the single most visible learner error at B1–B2.

sections.title: Coordinating conjunctions — FANBOYS
sections.content: The seven coordinating conjunctions (**for, and, nor, but, or, yet, so**) join **equal-rank** elements. When joining two independent clauses, put a **comma before the conjunction**; no comma for two words or short phrases.

items.good: I was tired, but I kept working.
items.why: two full clauses → comma before but

items.good: Hurry up, or you'll miss the train.
It was late, so we went home.

diagrams.text: Czech doesn't always require a comma before but / so / or in this pattern. In English written work, keep the comma when joining two clauses.

sections.title: Subordinating conjunctions — dependent clauses
sections.content: Subordinating conjunctions introduce a **dependent clause** — one that cannot stand alone. Word order decides the comma:

diagrams.title: Dependent clause position → comma
rows: Although it rained, we went out.
We went out although it rained.

items.good: Although it rained, we went out.
items.why: subordinate clause is not a sentence on its own

items.good: Because it rained. / Because of the rain.
items.why: 'because of' takes a noun, 'because' takes a clause

sections.title: Contrast — but / although / however / despite
sections.content: Four ways to mark contrast, **four different grammar slots**. Slot errors here are the #1 source of comma mistakes at B1–B2 — and a staple of FCE/CAE Sentence Transformation.

diagrams.title: Contrast linkers — structure side by side
rows: I was tired, but I kept going.
Although I was tired, I kept going.
I was tired. However, I kept going.
Despite being tired, I kept going.

sections.title: Cause & result — because / so / therefore / due to
sections.content: **Cause** (the reason) and **result** (the consequence) use different words in different positions. Never use a cause word and a result word together in the same sentence.

diagrams.title: Cause vs result — slot and register

items.bad: Because it rained, so we stayed in.
items.good: Because it rained, we stayed in. / It rained, so we stayed in.
Because it rained. / Because of the rain.

sections.title: Addition, sequence & discourse markers (B2)
sections.content:
**Addition** — choose register:
- **also** (mid-sentence): *He also writes novels.*
- **in addition / furthermore / moreover** (sentence start + comma): formal written
- **not only … but also**: *Not only is it cheap, but it's also fast.*

**Sequence** — for ordering paragraphs: *first / first of all → second / next / then → finally / lastly*. Note: *at last* means "after a long wait" — don't use it for listing.

**Discourse markers** — shape the argument without adding facts:
- Emphasis: *in fact, indeed, actually*
- Contrast: *on the other hand, in contrast*
- Restatement: *in other words, that is to say*
- Concession: *admittedly, granted, of course*

diagrams.text: Many English discourse markers don't translate one-to-one from Czech. *In fact* is not *v faktu*. Learn them as ready-made chunks, not word-for-word translations.

items.bad: Although he tried, but he failed.
items.good: Although he tried, he failed. / He tried but he failed.
Despite the rain. / In spite of the rain.

items.bad: It was cold, however we went out.
items.good: It was cold. However, we went out.
items.why: however needs a new sentence (or semicolon), not just a comma

questions.question: Which is correct?

options.a: Because it rained, we stayed at home.
options.b: Because it rained, so we stayed at home.
options.c: Because it rained, therefore we stayed.
options.d: It rained, because we stayed at home.

questions.explanation: Don't use because and so (or therefore) in the same sentence. Use one: Because it rained, we stayed. Or: It rained so we stayed.

questions.question: Which is correct?

options.a: Although he tried, but he failed.
options.b: Although he tried, however he failed.
options.d: Although he tried, and he failed.

questions.explanation: Don't use although and but (or however) together. Use one: Although he tried, he failed. Or: He tried but he failed.

questions.question: Which linker fits? 'It was expensive. _____, we bought it.'
questions.explanation: However shows contrast (expensive vs we bought it). Therefore would mean 'because it was expensive we bought it' which is odd.

questions.question: Which fits? 'I'll call you _____ I arrive.'
questions.explanation: When = at the time (I arrive). While is for two ongoing actions; during needs a noun (during the meeting).

questions.question: Which fits? 'She likes tea, _____ he prefers coffee.' (contrast)
questions.explanation: Whereas (or while) compares two contrasting facts inside one sentence: She likes tea, whereas he prefers coffee.

questions.question: Which fits? 'I don't know _____ she agreed.'

options.d: Both a and b are correct.

questions.explanation: Whether and if can both introduce an indirect yes/no question (I don't know whether/if she agreed). Whether is often preferred in formal writing.

questions.question: Wrong: 'Because it was late, so we went home.' Correct the sentence.
answers: Because it was late, we went home.
It was late so we went home.
Because it was late, we went home
It was late so we went home
questions.explanation: Don't use because and so together. Use one or the other.

questions.question: Wrong: 'Although she was tired, but she continued.' Correct the sentence.
answers: Although she was tired, she continued.
She was tired but she continued.
Although she was tired, she continued
She was tired but she continued
questions.explanation: Don't use although and but together.

questions.question: Fill in: It was difficult. _____, we managed to finish. (contrast)
answers: However
questions.explanation: However shows contrast (difficult vs we managed).

questions.question: Fill in: He didn't study. _____, he failed the exam. (result)
answers: Therefore

questions.question: Fill in: I was reading _____ she was cooking. (at the same time)
answers: while

questions.question: Wrong: 'Because he was ill, therefore he stayed in bed.' Correct the sentence.
answers: Because he was ill, he stayed in bed.
He was ill so he stayed in bed.
Because he was ill, he stayed in bed
He was ill so he stayed in bed
questions.explanation: Don't use because and therefore together.

questions.question: Fill in: We need to hurry _____ we miss the train. (negative purpose / result to avoid)
answers: or
otherwise
or we will
otherwise we will
questions.explanation: Or (we'll) miss = otherwise we'll miss. Shows negative result to avoid.

questions.question: Fill in: The film was long. _____, it was very good.
answers: However

questions.question: Fill in: _____ you see him, tell him to call me.
answers: When
questions.explanation: When = at the time you see him.

questions.question: Fill in: I don't know _____ she's coming. (introducing a yes/no alternative – one word)
answers: whether
if
questions.explanation: Whether and if can both introduce an indirect yes/no question (whether/if she's coming or not).

questions.question: Fill in: _____ it was expensive, we decided to buy it. (contrast)
answers: Although
questions.explanation: Although introduces a contrast (expensive vs we bought it).
